coredata.dc:title: A facile “off–on” fluorescence sensor for pentachlorophenol detection based on natural N and S co-doped carbon dots from crawfish shells
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Jiannan
Xia, Xiuhua
Li, Peizhen
Yu, Hang
Xie, Yunfei
Guo, Yahui
Yao, Weirong
Qian, He
Cheng, Yuliang
coredata.dc:description: Natural N and S co-doped carbon dots (NSCDs) were synthesized via hydrothermal synthesis using food-derived crawfish shells as green precursors. The relationship between quantum yield (QY) and protein content of six crawfish shells treated with different alkalis was investigated. The synthesized NSCDs displayed a large QY of 18.57 %, which was higher than most of the chitin-derived CDs. Due to the oxidation of hydroxyl radicals, adding H2O2 and horseradish peroxidase (HRP) quenched the fluorescence of NSCDs, resulting in a detection limit (LOD) of 1.25 μM for H2O2. The addition of pentachlorophenol (PCP) was supposed to consume part of the hydroxyl radicals and protect NSCDs from being oxidized. Accordingly, a facile and rapid “off–on” fluorescence sensor was developed to detect PCP with a LOD of 2.30 μM. Further, the method described was successfully used to detect PCP in three real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Crawfish shells
Protein
“Off–on” fluorescence sensor
Pentachlorophenol

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of NSCDs from crawfish shells
Determination of PCP in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Collaborative innovation center of food safety and quality control in Jiangsu Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work reported in this article was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (Project No. 2019YFC1606000), and Collaborative innovation center of food safety and quality control in Jiangsu Province.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image, (B) nanoparticle diameter distribution, (C) XRD pattern, (D) Raman spectrum of NSCDs.
(A) FTIR spectrum, (B) XPS survey spectrum, (C) C1s spectrum, (D) N1s spectrum, (E) O1s spectrum, and (F) S2p spectrum of the NSCDs.

(A) UV- absorption spectra, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of NSCDs. The inset exhibited NSCDs under sunlight and 360 nm UV light. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of NSCDs at different excitation wavelengths.
Fluorescence spectra of NSCDs, NSCDs + H2O2, NSCDs + HRP, NSCDs + PCP, NSCDs + H2O2 + HRP, and NSCDs + H2O2 + HRP + PCP, respectively.

(A) Fluorescence response of CDs/HRP/H2O2/PCP system with different PCP concentrations (10–300 μM). (B) The relationship between F/F0 and PCP concentration ranging from 10 to 300 µM. (C) N, S-CDs fluorescence response in the presence of PCP or competitive compounds.
Analytical results of PCP detection in fresh crawfish, aquaculture water and lake water samples based on the NSCDs@HRP/H2O2 system.

head.ce:title: A facile “off–on” fluorescence sensor for pentachlorophenol detection based on natural N and S co-doped carbon dots from crawfish shells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology, School of Food Science and Technology, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology
School of Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology, School of Food Science and Technology, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Collaborative Innovation Center of Food Safety and Quality Control in Jiangsu Province, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center of Food Safety and Quality Control in Jiangsu Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Collaborative Innovation Center of Food Safety and Quality Control in Jiangsu Province, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Wuxi Vocational Institute of Commerce, Wuxi 214122, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Wuxi Vocational Institute of Commerce, Wuxi 214122, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Food Science and Technology, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Natural N and S co-doped carbon dots (NSCDs) were synthesized via hydrothermal synthesis using food-derived crawfish shells as green precursors. The relationship between quantum yield (QY) and protein content of six crawfish shells treated with different alkalis was investigated. The synthesized NSCDs displayed a large QY of 18.57 %, which was higher than most of the chitin-derived CDs. Due to the oxidation of hydroxyl radicals, adding H2O2 and horseradish peroxidase (HRP) quenched the fluorescence of NSCDs, resulting in a detection limit (LOD) of 1.25 μM for H2O2. The addition of pentachlorophenol (PCP) was supposed to consume part of the hydroxyl radicals and protect NSCDs from being oxidized. Accordingly, a facile and rapid “off–on” fluorescence sensor was developed to detect PCP with a LOD of 2.30 μM. Further, the method described was successfully used to detect PCP in three real samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Pentachlorophenol (PCP) is one of the most common carcinogens and genotoxins detected in food. PCP is extensively used as a synthetic intermediate in disinfectant, pesticide, herbicide and wood preservative manufacture (Ren, Ma, Lee, Han, Cui, Wang, et al., 2021; Ye, Huang, Yang, Li, Zhuang, & Gu, 2019). The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has designated PCP as a priority pollutant due to its severe toxicity (Khuzwayo & Chirwa, 2017). Additionally, PCP is highly stable in the environment and accumulates in organisms through the food chain, thus posing a serious threat to food safety and human life. Hence, rapid and sensitive analytical methods are urgently needed to monitor trace levels of PCP.

Currently, spectrophotometry (Li, Zhao, Shi, Cai, Mou, & Jiang, 2008), gas chromatography (GC) (Zhou, Wu, Lin, Liang, Long, Lv, et al., 2021), gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) (Valdez, Salazar, & Leif, 2022), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (C. Fan, Li, & Cao, 2015), electrochemistry (Nesakumar, Sethuraman, Krishnan, & Rayappan, 2016) and other analytical techniques have been developed for PCP detection. However, most of these methods involve the use of expensive instruments, and the sample preparation is time-consuming and complicated (Nsibande & Forbes, 2016). Fluorescent probes based on various fluorescence sensing platforms are inexpensive, highly sensitive, simple to operate, and highly biocompatible, and therefore extensively used.

Carbon dots (CDs) are newly developed carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials, which are widely utilized to construct fluorescence sensors for the detection of various contaminants such as pesticides, drugs and heavy metals (Devi, Rajput, Thakur, Kim, & Kumar, 2019; Yusuf, Atulbhai, Swapna, Malek, & Kailasa, 2022). Compared with conventional semiconductor quantum dots, CDs are highly water soluble and stable, strongly biocompatible with low toxicity, easy functionalization, and enhanced photobleaching resistance (Wan, Wang, Zhang, Fu, Gao, Wang, et al., 2019; Zhu, Zhou, Liu, Luo, Du, Fan, et al., 2021). In the past decades, a variety of CDs synthesized from different precursors were used as fluorescent probes for the analysis of phenolic pollutants in real samples. Although these CDs were utilized in detecting phenolic pollutants, the precursors used were usually chemical reagents such as ethylenediamine (Han, Liu, Liang, Ju, Li, & Luo, 2019), norepinephrine (Fan, Tang, Liu, Yang, Ju, Xiao, et al., 2019), and 5-amino-1,10-phenanthroline (Zhao, Zhang, Liu, & Lü, 2020). Compared with chemical carbon sources, natural materials are less expensive, more environmentally friendly and can be produced on a large scale. Currently, various green raw materials such as lemon juice (Kaur, Mehta, & Kansal, 2017), apple seeds (Chatzimarkou, Chatzimitakos, Kasouni, Sygellou, Avgeropoulos, & Stalikas, 2018) and orange peels (Wang, Shi, Gao, Zhang, Deng, Fu, et al., 2020) are employed as precursors to prepare CDs. The green CDs exhibit outstanding fluorescence and detection. Interestingly, element doping is induced by raising the Fermi level and the electron in the conduction band position to improve the fluorescence of CDs (Liu, Ding, Zhang, & Ding, 2019). In recent years, magnetic metal ions doped into CDs nanostructures can improve the physical chemistry and magnetic properties of CDs, which is beneficial to expand their application in environmental analytical chemistry and biomedical sciences (Kailasa & Koduru, 2022). Nonmetallic elements doping is another satisfactory method used to regulate the fluorescence of CDs. N contains five valence electrons that bond with carbon atoms, and N atoms can rectify the surface defects of CDs, resulting in improved QY (Yuanyuan Liu, Jiang, Li, Fan, Wang, Liu, et al., 2019). In addition to single N doping, the doping effect is enhanced when N is combined with elements such as S or phosphorus (Babar & Garje, 2020; Mondal, Dinda, & Saha, 2018). The crawfish shells which originate from food wastes are naturally enriched in N elements and proteins. The N and S elements in crawfish shells can be incorporated into CDs via hydrothermal carbonization (Wang, Zheng, Wang, Chen, Xu, Zuo, et al., 2014). Thus, crawfish shells represent ideal green precursors for eco-friendly synthesis of CDs cost-effectively.

Herein, we developed a sustainable and economic strategy for the preparation of NSCDs via hydrothermal synthesis using crawfish shells as green precursors. Compared with the approaches using chitin as the precursor, this synthetic method recycles crawfish shells and obviates the need for hazardous reagents or complex synthetic procedures. Thus, the method is environmentally safe and scalable. Six crawfish shells treated with different alkalis were used to investigate the relationship between QY and protein content. The highly reactive hydroxyl radicals generated in Fenton reaction destroy the emission groups of CDs and effectively quench the fluorescence from the carbon nucleus (Wu, Pan, Ye, Liang, & Zhao, 2022). According to this theory, the active group on the surface of NSCDs is oxidized by the hydroxyl radicals generated in the reaction between H2O2 and HRP, leading to fluorescence quenching. However, the PCP competes with NSCDs and consumes a portion of the hydroxyl radicals to prevent their oxidation. Therefore, the NSCDs@HRP/H2O2 system can be used to develop a selective and sensitive “off–on” fluorescence sensor for PCP detection.

Crawfish shells were purchased from the local market (Wuxi, China). The analytical reagents, i.e., Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4, HCl, NaOH, quinine sulfate (QS), HRP, H2O2 (30 %), NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, MnCl2, MgCl2, CuCl2 and the test anions were supplied by the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Phenol, catechol, hydroquinone, p-nitrophenol, p-chlorophenol, 2,4,6-trichlorophenol, hexachlorobenzene, hexabromobenzene, bisphenol A and PCP were provided by Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All the chemicals were used at the least level of analysis. All aqueous solutions were prepared in ultrapure water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of NSCDs from crawfish shells
ce:section.ce:para: The crawfish shells were rinsed with tap water, followed by cleaning with deionized water. The cleaned crawfish shells were dried at 70 °C. The dried samples were pulverized and sifted through an 80-mesh sieve. The dried crawfish shell powder was treated with 0.2 M HCl (1:20 w/v) for 2 h ultrasonically, and then washed with ultrapure water to neutral pH. The crawfish shell powder was then transferred into 1 M NaOH (1:20 w/v) at 80 °C and treated for different times (0, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, and 2.5 h). The crawfish shell powder was then washed with ultrapure water to neutral pH.

NSCDs were synthesized via a facile and convenient hydrothermal synthesis from crawfish shells. The crawfish shell powder (1 g) was added to 25 mL of ultrapure water and treated with ultrasound for 10 min. Next, the mixture was transferred to a 45 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated for 6 h at 200 °C. The obtained solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μM hydrophilic membrane and further dialyzed for 48 h in a cellulose membrane cut to 1000 Da molecular weight. Finally, the purified brown solution was stored at 4 °C for further characterization and application.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of NSCDs were acquired using a JEM-2100 apparatus (JEOL Ltd., Japan) working at 200 kV. The size of NSCDs was recorded on a ZS-90 Zetasizer Nano (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) study of NSCDs was performed on a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker Instruments Ltd., Germany). The Raman spectrum was acquired on a Thermo Scientific™ DXR SmartRaman spectrometer (Thermo Scientific). The Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was obtained between 4000 and 500 cm−1 on a Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer (Nicolet Ltd., U.S.A.). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were acquired on an Escalab 250Xi XPS (Thermo Scientific). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption and fluorescence spectra were measured using UV-1800 and RF-6000 spectrophotometers (Shimadzu Ltd., Japan).

NSCDs (10 mg) were added to 1000 mL of PBS solution (pH = 5.0) to form a 10 mg L−1 dispersion, followed by the addition of 100 μL of HRP (20 nM) and 100 μL of H2O2 with different concentrations (5–500 μM) into 800 μL of the NSCD dispersion. The mixture was shaken thoroughly and then allowed to react at 30 °C for 40 min. The fluorescence spectra with a recording emission range of 380–650 nm were collected at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. To explore the selectivity of H2O2, the above NSCDs@HRP system was supplemented with K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, F−, Cl−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, SO4 2− and H2O2 separately.

An NSCDs@HRP/H2O2 system containing 800 μL of the above NSCD dispersion, 100 μL of HRP (20 nM) and 100 μL of H2O2 (300 μM) in a centrifuge tube was treated with 100 μL of a specific concentration of PCP (10–300 μM). The mixture was reacted at 30 °C for 40 min, and the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Several common structurally similar aromatics were used to investigate the selectivity of NSCDs@HRP/H2O2 system towards PCP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of PCP in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Water samples were obtained from Taihu Lake and aquaculture water in Jiangsu. First, the water sample was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min, followed by 0.22 μm hydrophilic membrane filtration. Next, the obtained water samples were mixed with the standard PCP solution (0, 50, 100, and 150 μM) to obtain spiked samples. Different concentrations of PCP (0, 50, 100, and 150 μM) were added to 2 g of crawfish meat and mixed overnight. The mixtures were crushed thoroughly with a homogenizer and dispersed into 6 mL of 5 % triethylamine acetonitrile aqueous solution. The samples were vortexed for 1 min, followed by ultrasonic extraction for 5 min and centrifugation. After centrifugation, the residue was repeatedly treated with 6 mL of 5 % triethylamine acetonitrile-aqueous solution, and the supernatant was combined and mixed. Finally, the levels of PCP in water samples and crawfish meat samples were determined using the NSCDs@HRP/H2O2 system.

Compared with traditional synthesis, the hydrothermal synthesis combined with polymerization and carbonization is easy to control, simple to operate, save energy and protect the environment (Liu, Ding, Zhang, & Ding, 2019). Hence, in this study, natural NSCDs were prepared from crawfish shells using a facile hydrothermal method. First, CDs derived from 6 crawfish shells treated with different alkalis were produced under similar synthetic conditions. As shown in Fig. S1 (Supplementary Materials), the QY of 6 CDs showed a large difference, and the CDs prepared from untreated crawfish shells generated the highest QY, which might be related to the disparate protein content of crawfish shells treated with different alkalis for varying durations. Thus, the retention of protein in crawfish shells is necessary to improve the fluorescence of CDs, which is inconsistent with the general mechanism (Gedda, Lee, Lin, & Wu, 2016). Therefore, the alkali treatment will not be adopted in subsequent experiments.

The reaction temperature, reaction time and solid–liquid ratio, were optimized first during the preparation of NSCDs. The fluorescence spectra of NSCDs were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. As shown in Fig. S2 (Supplementary Materials), when the reaction temperature (group: 120, 140, 160, 180, 200, 220 and 240 °C) was 200 °C, the NSCDs exhibited the maximum fluorescence intensity (Fig. S2
A&B). It is obvious that an increase in reaction temperature facilitated the generation of CDs. However, when the reaction temperature exceeded 200 °C, the carbon core developed rapidly. The small size effect was not obvious, and the uneven absorption efficiency led to a decline in fluorescence. The fluorescence intensity (Fig. S2C&D) peaked at the reaction time of 6 h (group: 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12 and 14 h). The maximum fluorescence intensity (Fig. S2E & F) was detected at the solid–liquid ratio of 1:25 (group: 1:10, 1:15, 1:20, 1:25, 1:30, 1:35 and 1:40). Crawfish shells (1 g) were used to prepare N, S-CDs as raw materials, using 25 mL ultrapure water as solvent, at a reaction temperature of 200 °C, and a reaction time of 6 h. Using quinine sulfate as the reference, the QY of NSCDs was 18.57 %.

The morphology, composition and functional groups of NSCDs prepared under optimum conditions were characterized by TEM, XRD, XPS, FT-IR and Raman spectroscopy. The spherical morphology of the dispersed NSCDs was revealed by TEM (Fig. 1A). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (inset in Fig. 1A) reveals that a small fraction of CDs had graphene-like lattice planes with lattice spacing of 0.2 nm. Fig. 1B shows the diameter of the particle size ranging from 3.0 nm to 5.5 nm and the mean size was 4.0 nm. The crystalline nature of the NSCDs was determined via XRD. As can be seen from Fig 1C, a wide diffraction peak was centered at 2θ = 22.3° in the XRD of N, S-CDs, which belonged to the height (002) surface of graphite carbon, indicating the presence of disordered carbon atoms. (Yinghui Liu, Zhu, Gao, Yang, Xu, Zhang, et al., 2020). The Raman spectrum (Fig. 1D) shows a disordered d-band at 1367 cm−1 associated with sp3 defect and a stronger crystal G-band at 1590 cm−1 associated with sp2 carbon in-plane vibration. The intensity ratio of ID/IG was 0.82, suggesting the existence of defective carbon and amorphous structures in NSCDs (Hu & Gao, 2020). The Raman spectrum was consistent with XRD analysis.

The FTIR spectrum and XPS analysis were performed to investigate the functional groups and elemental composition of the NSCDs. As shown in Fig. 2A, the peaks at 3230 cm−1 and 1410 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH/NH and CN, respectively, demonstrating N doping of NSCDs (H. Wang, Lu, Hou, Liu, & Zhang, 2016). The band at 2954 cm−1 was assigned to the CH stretching vibration. Additionally, the sharp band at 1668 cm−1 belonged to the CO functional group. The CC stretching vibrations at 1592 cm−1 and 1450 cm−1 indicated that the NSCDs contained polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons. The characteristic band at 1120 cm−1 confirmed the existence of a CS bond (Ahmed, Iqbal, Zhao, & Xiong, 2021). The FTIR study was confirmed by the XPS analysis. The XPS full-scan spectra (Fig. 2B) showed four distinct peaks at 285.1, 400.1, 531.1, and 163.1 eV belonging to C1s, N1s, O1s, and S2p, respectively. The relative levels of elements were 66.82 % C, 12.7 % N, 20.01 % O, and 0.47 % S, respectively. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 2C) was decomposed into three major component bands at 284.6 eV, 286.1 eV, and 287.8 eV, which were related to CC, CO, and CO, respectively. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2D) exhibited three bands at 398.4 eV, 399.5 eV, and 400.6 eV, representing the three different N species, i.e., pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N (Hu & Gao, 2020). The O1s spectrum (Fig. 2E) showed three prominent bands at 530.5 eV, 531.2 eV, and 532.4 eV, respectively, matching COH, CO, and COC, respectively. The S2p spectrum (Fig. 2F) was integrated into the two bands at 163.7 eV and 168.3 eV, which were attributed to CS and C-SOX, respectively (Ahmed, Iqbal, Zhao, & Xiong, 2021). The results of FTIR and XPS analysis suggested that the as-received NSCDs were oxygen-functionalized and co-doped with N and S, leading to excellent water solubility and reactivity.

UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were performed to analyze the optical properties of NSCDs, such as absorption, excitation, emission. The absorption spectrum (blue line, Fig. 3 A) of NSCDs showed two characteristic peaks centered at 273 nm and 364 nm, attributed to the π-π* transition with CC and the n-π* transition with CO on the surface of CDs (Sun, Wang, Li, Shao, Xia, Liu, et al., 2022). As shown in Fig. 3A, the maximum emission wavelength of NSCDs was 440 nm and the optimal excitation wavelength was 360 nm. The NSCD solution was achromatous under daytime illumination and exhibited bright blue fluorescence upon excitation at 360 nm (inset of Fig. 3A). Fig. 3B shows different emission spectra of NSCDs measured at excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 nm to 400 nm, and excitation-dependent fluorescence was observed. As the excitation wavelength increased from 300 nm to 400 nm, the fluorescence emission peak was red-shifted from 396 nm to 464 nm. This excitation-dependent fluorescence was attributed to the different size distribution in the NSCDs solution and the existence of various surface functional groups of NSCDs (Hu & Gao, 2020; Wan, et al., 2019).

The stability of NSCDs was tested at different pH values, ionic strength and illumination times and their effect on the fluorescence was assessed, as described in Supplementary Materials (Fig. S3). Fig. S3A shows that the fluorescence intensity of NS-CDs did not change substantially when the pH ranged from 4 to 9, but decreased significantly when the pH turned acidic (pH < 4) or alkaline (pH > 9). This phenomenon may be associated with protonation/deprotonation and charge transfer of surface groups such as carboxyl and amino (Sun, et al., 2022). Fig. S3B demonstrates the effect of different concentrations of NaCl (0–5 M) on the fluorescence of NSCDs. When the ionic strength was increased, the fluorescence intensity of NSCDs was basically stable, which demonstrated that the ionic strength had little effect on the fluorescence intensity. Further, the effect of illumination time on the fluorescence intensity of NSCDs was also investigated. As depicted in Fig. S3C, the fluorescence intensity of NSCDs showed no significant difference under continuous excitation (360 nm) for 12 h with a UV lamp, which demonstrated that the NSCDs showed strong photostability.

The effects of H2O2, HRP and PCP on the fluorescence intensity of NSCDs were investigated as shown in Fig. 4 . No significant variation was detected in the fluorescence intensity of NSCDs treated with H2O2, HRP or PCP separately. However, the fluorescence of NSCDs was obviously quenched by the combination of H2O2 and HRP. Further, the addition of a specific number of PCP molecules into NSCDs@HRP/H2O2 system partially restored the fluorescence intensity, as shown in Fig. 4. This phenomenon was due to the competition by PCP with NSCDs and consumption of a portion of hydroxyl radicals to prevent their oxidation. Accordingly, the synthesized NSCDs can be used to develop a facile fluorescent switching sensor for PCP detection in water and crawfish samples.

To further improve the efficiency of NSCDs in H2O2 and PCP detection, we optimized the detection parameters such as the reaction time, pH, and the amount of HRP. The results are shown in Fig. S4 (Supplementary Materials). As indicated in Fig. S4A, the fluorescence intensity of the NSCDs@H2O2/HRP system decreased rapidly during the first 30 min, and remained stable after 40 min. Thus, the reaction time of 40 min was selected in subsequent experiments. Experiments were performed at different pH values of 4.0–9.0 to investigate the effect of altered pH on the fluorescence intensity of the detection system. As shown in Fig. S4B, the NSCDs@H2O2/HRP fluorescence intensity was the lowest at pH 5.0. Hence, pH 5.0 was selected as the optimal pH in subsequent experiments. In addition, the effect of HRP concentration on the NSCDs@H2O2/HRP fluorescence intensity was investigated. As shown in Fig. S4C, the addition of HRP decreased the fluorescence intensity of NSCDs@H2O2/HRP system significantly, and when the HRP level exceeded 20 nM, the fluorescence intensity tended to be stable and no longer changed. Thus, 20 nM HRP was selected for subsequent experiments.

The analytical performance of the sensing system was investigated by measuring the fluorescence spectra of the NSCDs@H2O2/HRP system using a series of H2O2 concentrations under optimal conditions. As depicted in Fig. S5 (Supplementary Materials), a gradual decrease in the NSCDs@H2O2/HRP fluorescence intensity was observed with increasing H2O2 concentration. The inset in Fig. S5 B illustrated that F/F0 exhibited a strong linear relationship with H2O2 concentrations ranging from 5 µM to 100 µM. The LOD was calculated to be 1.25 µM, which was comparable with previously reported values as shown in Table S1 (Supplementary Materials). In addition, to determine the selectivity of the proposed method for H2O2 detection, the changes in fluorescence intensity of the detection system were measured in the presence of various ions. As shown clearly in Fig. S5C, the effect of K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, F−, Cl−, NO3 −, CO3 2− and SO4 2− on the NSCD fluorescence intensity was negligible, suggesting that our proposed method had strong specificity for H2O2 for potential practical application.

The addition of PCP to NSCDs@HRP/H2O2 system restored the fluorescence, suggesting that the NSCDs@HRP/H2O2 system can be utilized as a novel turn-on strategy for fluorescent detection of PCP. As shown in Fig. 5A, with the increase of PCP concentrations from 0 to 300 μM, the NSCDs@HRP/H2O2/PCP fluorescence was gradually strengthened. The F/F0 and PCP concentrations (10–300 μM) exhibited good linear relationship (Fig. 5B, R2 = 0.9981). The LOD was calculated to be 2.30 μM. The analytical performance of NSCDs@HRP/H2O2 system to PCP, including linear range and LOD, was compared with the previous works in Table S2 (Supplementary Materials). It can be found that the proposed method combined the advantages of high sensitivity, excellent specificity, low cost and sustainable material preparation process. To determine the selectivity of NSCDs@HRP/H2O2 system, control experiments were carried out to analyze various structurally similar aromatic compounds, such as phenol, catechol, hydroquinone, p-nitrophenol, p-chlorophenol, 2,4,6-trichlorophenol, hexachlorobenzene, hexabromobenzene and bisphenol A. As shown in Fig. 5C and Table S3, these competitive compounds, except that p-nitrophenol (p-NP) had an effect on fluorescence recovery, did not significantly affect the fluorescence intensity of NS-CDs/HRP/H2O2 system, indicating the excellent selectivity of the system for PCP.

It can be seen from Fig. 4, the fluorescence of NSCDs was significantly quenched by the addition of H2O2 and HRP. This phenomenon could be attributed to the fact that HRP (a class of heme-containing enzymes) acts as a biological Fenton catalyst to produce hydroxyl radicals as active substances in the reaction with H2O2 (Barati, Shamsipur, & Abdollahi, 2015; H. Li, Li, Cao, Li, & Zhang, 2011). As shown in Fig. 2, the FTIR spectrum and XPS analysis indicated that the NSCDs carried a number of surface functional groups such as –OH, –NH2 and CS, which played a significant role in the formation of emissive traps and surface states. The surface reductive nitrogen, sulfur and oxygen-containing groups of NSCDs were oxidized by the highly reactive hydroxyl radicals generated in the reaction between H2O2 and HRP, which changed the surface states and induced fluorescence quenching (Barati, Shamsipur, & Abdollahi, 2015; Wu, Pan, Ye, Liang, & Zhao, 2022). In Fig. S6 (Supplementary Materials), the lifetime of NSCDs, NSCDs@HRP/H2O2 were 8.38 ns and 5.95 ns, respectively. The decrease of fluorescence lifetime was an obvious feature of dynamic quenching (Wu, Pan, Ye, Liang, & Zhao, 2022). Therefore, after the addition of H2O2 and HRP, the fluorescence of NSCDs is turn ‘off’.

It is generally assumed that the hydroxyl radicals can react with aromatic compounds via double bonds, hydrogen atom substitution (if a side group -CnHmX is present in the molecule) or electron transfer (Oturan, Oturan, Lahitte, & Trevin, 2001). In the case of PCP, chlorine atoms dominate the rest of the aromatic cycle. Therefore, it was speculated that hydroxyl radicals attack the chlorine residues and release chloride ions in the process (Yuan & Lu, 2005). In the presence of the hydroxyl radicals, the primary hydroxylated products of PCP, i.e., tetrachloro-catechol and tetrachloro-hydroquinone are oxidized to tetrachloro-quinone and tetrachloro-p-benzoquinone, respectively (Yuan & Lu, 2005; Zimbron & Reardon, 2009). In order to explore the mechanism of fluorescence recovery after the addition of PCP, the fluorescence of NSCDs after the addition of PCP followed by H2O2 and HRP was measured. As shown in Fig. S7 (Supplementary Materials), the fluorescence of NSCDs did not change when PCP was added first. This phenomenon indicated that PCP provided a reducing environment that can protect the active group of NSCDs from oxidation. Thus, the fluorescence of NSCDs was turned on when PCP was added to the NSCDs@HRP/H2O2 system.

To evaluate the practical application of the NSCDs@HRP/H2O2 system, experiments were conducted to measure the PCP concentration in fresh crawfish, aquaculture water and lake water samples. The results of spiked recovery experiment are presented in Table 1 . The PCP recovery ranged from 95.62 % to 105.17 %, with a maximum relative standard deviation (RSD) of 4.50 %, indicating that the proposed fluorescence method exhibited significant potential for PCP determination in real samples.

In summary, a sustainable strategy for NSCD synthesis was developed using a single-step green hydrothermal method utilizing food-derived crawfish shells as green precursors. Treating six crawfish shells with different alkalis, we first found that the QY of the CDs derived from crawfish shells was related to the protein content in crawfish shells. The synthesized NSCDs generated a high QY of 18.57 % and a high sensitivity for H2O2, with an LOD of 1.25 μM, which suggested strong feasibility for the detection of H2O2. Besides, based on fluorescence quenching experiments, the NSCDs@HRP/H2O2 system can be used as a “off–on” fluorescence sensor for PCP measurement with an LOD of 2.30 µM. Further, the efficiency and potential for practical application of this method for PCP determination was further demonstrated using fresh crawfish, aquaculture water and lake water samples. Compared with previous studies, the current study provides a simple, cost-effective and advanced fluorescent nanosensor with enhanced performance for effective detection of PCP in real samples.

Jiannan Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Xiuhua Xia: Investigation, Data curation, Visualization. Peizhen Li: Resources, Formal analysis, Software. Hang Yu: Resources, Software. Yunfei Xie: Validation. Yahui Guo: Resources. Weirong Yao: Writing – review & editing. He Qian: Supervision. Yuliang Cheng: Project administration, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work reported in this article was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (Project No. 2019YFC1606000), and Collaborative innovation center of food safety and quality control in Jiangsu Province.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2022.134802.
